title: Enterprise agreements

meta.description: How enterprise agreements work in Australia, the Better Off Overall Test (BOOT), and individual flexibility arrangements.

content:
An enterprise agreement is a legally binding agreement between an employer and a group of employees (or a union on their behalf) that sets out the terms and conditions of employment at the enterprise level.

Enterprise agreements can provide different conditions from the applicable Modern Award — they can provide more than the award, or different conditions, as long as the employees are better off overall.

How enterprise agreements are made:
1. Bargaining — employer and employees (often through unions) negotiate terms
2. Employee vote — a majority of employees who will be covered must vote in favour
3. Fair Work Commission approval — the FWC must approve the agreement before it comes into force

Better Off Overall Test (BOOT):
The FWC applies the BOOT to assess whether employees are better off overall under the proposed enterprise agreement compared to the applicable Modern Award. The agreement must pass the BOOT — meaning the overall terms must not leave employees worse off.

The BOOT considers:
- All monetary and non-monetary entitlements
- Conditions in the enterprise agreement vs award conditions
- It is assessed for each class of employee covered

An enterprise agreement can trade off some award conditions (e.g. lower penalty rates for a higher base rate) as long as employees are better off overall.

Types of enterprise agreements:
1. Single-enterprise agreements — cover one employer
2. Multi-enterprise agreements — cover multiple employers (various types introduced by recent reforms)

Relationship with awards:
- An enterprise agreement replaces the applicable award for the duration of the agreement
- The NES still applies — an enterprise agreement cannot exclude or diminish NES entitlements
- If the enterprise agreement expires, the award applies again until a new agreement is reached

Individual Flexibility Arrangements (IFAs):
Most awards and enterprise agreements include an IFA clause that allows an employer and individual employee to agree to vary certain conditions. Requirements:
- Must be in writing
- Employee must be genuinely better off overall
- Employee can terminate the IFA with 28 days' notice
- IFA cannot undercut NES entitlements

Awards that allow IFAs typically let parties vary hours of work, overtime rates, penalty rates, allowances, and leave arrangements — but only if the employee is better off overall.
tables: Yes — employees must be better off overall
Must comply — NES cannot be excluded
